mechanism: Proposed mechanisms include effects of gingerols/shogaols on gastrointestinal motility and nausea signaling pathways. These mechanisms are plausible but do not establish uniform clinical benefit across all uses.
effects: No direct effects data. Contextual inference: Folk medicine.. No direct mechanismofaction data. Contextual inference: Folk medicine.. Folk medicine. Folk medicine
contraindications: Avoid during pregnancy and breastfeeding unless supervised by a qualified clinician.
Do not use with known allergy to this plant or related species.
safetyNotes: Ginger has been used safely in many oral studies. GI upset can occur, and people using prescription medicines should check for potential herb-drug interactions before routine use.
description: Ginger rhizome is used as food and as an oral supplement. NCCIH notes substantial clinical research, but study quality varies by indication and product type.
dosage: No single standardized dose is established across indications; clinical trials use product-specific regimens.

interactions: Potential pharmacologic interaction risk with some medicines; review use with a clinician or pharmacist if taking chronic medications.
preparation: Common forms include fresh or dried rhizome, teas, powders, capsules, and standardized extracts.
region: Widely cultivated globally; the species is traditionally associated with South and Southeast Asia.
therapeuticUses: Traditional supportive use; indication depends on species part and preparation.
Evidence level varies; use only within established clinical or traditional context.
sideEffects: May cause gastrointestinal upset (nausea, cramping, or diarrhea).
Possible headache, dizziness, or allergic skin reaction in sensitive users.
summary: Ginger (Zingiber officinale) is commonly used for nausea-related symptoms. Evidence is strongest for pregnancy-related nausea; findings are mixed for chemotherapy- or surgery-related nausea.
